column1: Key Linguistic Family Relationships
Indo-European Branch
Proto-Indo-European → Proto-Indo-Iranian → Proto-Iranian → Avestan
Proto-Indo-European → Proto-Indo-Iranian → Proto-Indo-Aryan → Vedic Sanskrit Proto-Indo-European → Proto-Greek → Mycenaean Greek (Linear B) → Ancient Greek dialects
Semitic Branch
Proto-Afroasiatic → Proto-Semitic → Proto-Northwest Semitic → Proto-Canaanite → Hebrew & Phoenician Proto-Afroasiatic → Proto-Semitic → Proto-Northwest Semitic → Proto-Aramaic → Aramaic dialects
Script Evolution Connections
Proto-Sinaitic (c. 2000-1500 BCE) → Phoenician alphabet (c. 1200-1000 BCE) →
Hebrew script (c. 1000 BCE onward)
Greek alphabet (c. 800 BCE onward)
Aramaic script (c. 800 BCE onward) → Avestan alphabet (c. 300-400 CE)
Notes on Avestan-Sanskrit Relationship
Old Avestan is extraordinarily close to Vedic Sanskrit in both grammar and lexicon, indicating that:
The split between Proto-Iranian and Proto-Indo-Aryan occurred relatively shortly before the composition of the oldest texts in both languages
Both languages preserved a remarkably conservative linguistic structure from their Proto-Indo-Iranian ancestor
The similarities suggest that speakers of both languages may have maintained some degree of mutual intelligibility
Geographic Distribution of Avestan
Eastern parts of Greater Iran, corresponding to modern Afghanistan, Tajikistan, and parts of Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan
No specific archaeological culture can be definitively linked, though the Yaz culture has been suggested due to its:
Connection with southward spread of steppe-derived Iranian groups
Agricultural practices consistent with Young Avestan society
Lack of burial sites, potentially indicating Zoroastrian practice of open-air excarnation
